career.notes: First law practice after Williams College 1820.
Returned to Massachusetts; practiced law at South Hadley. Met and married Mary Ann Dwight here 1834.

Per obituary: 'lawyery at D.H. Lamb & Co.' EH 1909 says 'engaged in soda manufacture' — D.H. Lamb & Co. may have been the soda manufacturing enterprise for which Edward served as legal counsel and commercial partner. Removed from Rochester NY to Cleveland 1835; remained until his death August 1, 1841 at thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine; wife outlived him fifty years
narrative_blocks.body: Edward left West Hampton MA as a Williams College graduate, practiced law in Ohio and Massachusetts, then pivoted to soda manufacturing in Cleveland — dying there August 1, 1841 at only thirty-nine, leaving two children under age seven. His wife Mary Ann Dwight survived him until June 4, 1891, outliving him by exactly fifty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Became, late in life, a man of prayer
narrative_blocks.body: The obituary that marked Edward's death at thirty-nine focused less on his law practice at D.H. Lamb & Co. than on the man he had become: late in life he united with the Congregational Church in Ohio City and was remembered by those who knew him simply as 'a man of prayer.' For a direct descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker — who helped establish the Congregational tradition in Connecticut two centuries earlier — the phrase carried the full weight of inheritance.

narrative_blocks.header: Williams 1820; youngest son of a man who
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker graduated Williams College in 1820 and practiced law at Ravenna Ohio, then South Hadley Massachusetts, before settling in Cleveland with D.H. Lamb & Co. He was the youngest son of Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton, who lived to ninety-four — Edward died at thirty-nine.

Returned to Massachusetts; practiced law at South Hadley. Met and married Mary Ann Dwight here 1834.

Per obituary: 'lawyery at D.H. Lamb & Co.' EH 1909 says 'engaged in soda manufacture' — D.H. Lamb & Co. may have been the soda manufacturing enterprise for which Edward served as legal counsel and commercial partner. Removed from Rochester NY to Cleveland 1835; remained until his death August 1, 1841 at thirty-nine.

Lamb & Co. than on the man he had become: late in life he united with the Congregational Church in Ohio City and was remembered by those who knew him simply as 'a man of prayer.' For a direct descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker — who helped establish the Congregational tradition in Connecticut two centuries earlier — the phrase carried the full weight of inheritance.

narrative_blocks.header: Williams 1820; youngest son of a man who
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker graduated Williams College in 1820 and practiced law at Ravenna Ohio, then South Hadley Massachusetts, before settling in Cleveland with D.H. Lamb & Co. He was the youngest son of Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton, who lived to ninety-four — Edward died at thirty-nine.

parents.father_research_notes: Elihu Dwight MD of South Hadley MA per EH 1909 #1415.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Williams College 1855; afterward studied medicine at East Cambridge.

cross_connections.display_label: East Cambridge physician and Massachusetts legislator, his medical mentor

narrative_blocks.header: Died studying medicine; disease contracted in hospital
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Williams College in 1855, Edward came to East Cambridge to study medicine under his uncle Dr. Anson Hooker — but died April 26, 1858 at twenty-three of disease contracted in hospital service, before completing his training. Six weeks earlier, his first cousin Edward Dwight had been born in the same household, beginning the physician career Edward would not live to pursue.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband died of yellow fever in Indiana, 1873
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Walworth Smith had been a merchant at St. Thomas in the West Indies before settling at Cincinnati and operating a wrecking business on the Ohio River. He died of yellow fever December 2, 1873 at Evansville Indiana, leaving Mary a widow at thirty-five with two young sons.

parents.father_research_notes: Erastus Ferrant Smith of Groton CT per EH 1909 #2764.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #2764: 'for some years a merchant at St. Thomas, W.I.'
Per EH: 'engaged in wrecking business upon the Ohio river' after settling Cincinnati.

naming_inspiration.description: Given names Edward Hooker honor maternal grandfather Edward Hooker (H01415) and carry the Hooker surname into the Smith line.
Middle name Dwight honors maternal grandmother Mary Ann Dwight Hooker, daughter of Elihu MD and Lydia White Dwight of South Hadley MA.
Middle name Church — origin not documented in EH; possibly honors a Church family connection. Research needed.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

burialCemetery.name: Monroe Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cleveland Cuyahoga County Ohio. One of Cleveland's oldest cemeteries, established 1836. Confirmed burial: H01415 Edward Hooker (1802-1841). Probable additional Hooker-tree burials: H01141 Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles (d.1861 Cleveland), HD3708 Giles Hooker Cowles (d.1842 Cleveland), I00396 Almira Mills Foote Cowles (d.1846 Cleveland) — all early Cleveland deaths with no cemetery wired; FGA verification needed. FindAGrave Cemetery #42187.

INST043.name: Williams College
INST043.notes: Private liberal arts college founded 1793 with funds from the estate of Ephraim Williams, a colonist killed in the French and Indian War (1755). One of the original "Little Three" liberal arts colleges.
